nativeRange.habitat: Inhabits deep recesses, caves, and overhangs of coral reefs; generally observed at depths of roughly 3–45 m. Described as common but secretive and rarely seen in the open.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a variety of meaty preparations in captivity, including mysis shrimp, finely chopped marine fish flesh, krill, and quality frozen carnivore mixes. Generally advised to feed once or twice daily, only what will be consumed within a few minutes. Tends to ambush small prey from cover rather than chase food in open water.

avoidWith: other Liopropoma basslets (unless mated pair in a larger system)
tiny gobies and other bite-sized fish
large aggressive predators (lionfish, large groupers, moray eels)
boisterous tankmates that out-compete for food

summary: A small, secretive Caribbean reef basslet (Liopropoma rubre) with bold red, yellow, and black banding. Generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe, but tends to spend most of its time tucked into caves and overhangs.
careNotes:
The Swissguard Basslet is a small, cave-dwelling predator from the tropical Western Atlantic. It tends to be highly secretive — described in field guides as common but rarely seen — and in the aquarium it spends most of its time hovering near a favored cave entrance or cruising the lower water column for prey. A mature aquascape with deep caves, overhangs, and shaded crevices is generally advised so the fish has somewhere it feels safe enough to come out and feed.

Generally considered reef-safe with the usual caveat: have been known to pick off very small ornamental shrimp and bite-sized fish, so avoid pairing with sexy shrimp, very small gobies, or post-larval livestock. Coral and larger invertebrates are typically left alone. Tends to be peaceful toward unrelated tankmates, but is intolerant of conspecifics and other Liopropoma basslets — best kept singly unless you can source a confirmed mated pair for a noticeably larger system.

Has been reported to be a notorious jumper, especially when startled; a tightly fitted lid is generally advised. Tank-size guidance varies widely across sources (LiveAquaria 10 gal, AquariumDomain and Quality Marine 30 gal), but the species' need for rockwork territory and an established footprint of caves makes the larger recommendation (~120 L) the more defensible floor.

Cross-source notes: family placement is given as Liopropomatidae by FishBase (the current accepted classification, formerly Serranidae subfamily Liopropomatinae) while some retailer pages list Grammidae — Grammidae is incorrect for this genus and the FishBase placement is used here. Adult size sources span 7.6 cm (most retailers' 3-inch figure) to 10 cm TL (FishBase max); the range here reflects that spread. Temperature range varies: LiveAquaria and Quality Marine give 22.2–25.6°C, AquariumDomain 23.3–26.7°C, FishBase 25.4–28°C (mean 27.2); the averaged 23–27°C range is used.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for general husbandry baseline (water parameters, diet, temperament, reef-safe status). Listed family (Grammidae) was rejected in favor of FishBase's Liopropomatidae.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Liopropomatidae, order Perciformes), max body length (10.0 cm TL), depth range (3–45 m), native distribution, and field behavior (common but secretive).

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked min tank size (30 gal / ~114 L), temperament, diet, water parameters (74–80°F), reef-safe caveats, and cave-dwelling behavior. Designated secondary BlueZooAquatics returned 404; AquariumDomain substituted.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Additional cross-check for min tank size (30 gal), max size (4 in / ~10 cm), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), conspecific aggression note, and jumping/lid warning. Reef2Reef wiki, TFH Magazine, and Fishkeeping World pages for this species all returned 403/404.
